verses.text: Moses called Israel: hear the statutes I speak this day. The LORD made a covenant with us in Horeb — not with our fathers only, but with us, even us, who are all of us here alive this day. The LORD talked with you face to face out of the fire; I stood between the LORD and you, for ye were afraid. Guidance: 'Us, even us' — the new generation owns the covenant their parents heard. Faith is not inherited like furniture; each generation must say 'us.' Sinai is yesterday and today at once.

I am the LORD thy God, which brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage. Thou shalt have none other gods before Me. Guidance: The first sentence is grace; the second is the first command. He saves us BEFORE He asks anything. No other gods is not a starting demand — it is a reasonable response after a rescue. The fugitives owe the deliverer their loyalty.

Thou shalt not make any graven image. Thou shalt not bow down to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God, visiting iniquity of fathers upon children unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate Me — and shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love Me and keep My commandments. Guidance: He is jealous because He loves — and the proof is the math. Sin to the third and fourth generation, mercy to a thousand. The ratio between His anger and His mercy is not a tie. Mercy outweighs by a hundred-to-one.

Thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain: for the LORD will not hold him guiltless that taketh His name in vain. Guidance: A name is a person, given for use. To use God's name carelessly is to handle a holy thing like a doorknob. He will not hold the careless guiltless — even when no one heard. The mouth that speaks His name should know whose it is.

Keep the sabbath day to sanctify it. Six days shalt thou labour. The seventh is the sabbath of the LORD: thou shalt not do any work — son, daughter, manservant, maidservant, ox, stranger — that thy manservant and maidservant may rest as well as thou. Remember thou wast a servant in Egypt; the LORD brought thee out: therefore the LORD commanded thee to keep the sabbath. Guidance: A different reason than Exodus 20. There the sabbath remembers creation; here it remembers slavery. The ex-slave keeps the day so HER slave can rest. Memory of bondage becomes mercy to others. Sabbath is freedom shared, not freedom hoarded.

Honour thy father and thy mother, as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee; that thy days may be prolonged, and that it may go well with thee, in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee. Guidance: The first commandment with a promise. The honour given to those who gave you a name has a way of lengthening the giver. Society where parents are revered tends to stand longer; God writes this into the architecture of nations.

Thou shalt not kill. Neither shalt thou commit adultery. Neither shalt thou steal. Neither shalt thou bear false witness against thy neighbour. Neither shalt thou desire thy neighbour's wife, neither shalt thou covet thy neighbour's house, his field, manservant, maidservant, ox, ass, or any thing that is thy neighbour's. Guidance: Five sentences that protect every neighbour's body, bed, goods, name, and contentment. The last commandment guards the heart — coveting is the seedbed of the first four. Stop the wanting and most of the harm never happens.

These words the LORD spake out of the fire, cloud, thick darkness — and added no more. He wrote them on two tables of stone. When ye heard the voice, ye said: the LORD hath shewed us His glory; we have heard His voice — this day we have seen God doth talk with man, and he liveth. Why should we die? Go thou near, hear all the LORD shall say; we will hear and do. Guidance: They had a hot mic with God and asked to mute it. Holiness up close is unbearable. They beg for a mediator — and Moses agrees, foreshadowing the One who would stand between God and humanity forever.

The LORD: I have heard the words this people have spoken — they have well said. O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear Me and keep all My commandments always, that it might be well with them and their children for ever! Go say to them, get to your tents. But thou, stand here by Me, I will speak all the commandments thou shalt teach them. Guidance: 'O that there were such an heart.' The most poignant divine sigh in the Pentateuch. God Himself wishes His people would love Him from inside. The promise of Jeremiah 31 — a new heart — was already on His lips by the foot of Sinai.
